comments.text: I would like to point out a small observation about the weakened question mentioned in [Er88c], namely the existence of a rational number \(x\) which has two representations. Interpreting a representation as a choice of a set \(A\subseteq \mathbb N\) of distinct positive integers, so that\[ x=\sum_{a\in A}\frac{a}{2^a}, \]one gets such an example from the finite identity\[ \frac{4}{2^4}=\frac{5}{2^5}+\frac{6}{2^6}. \]Both sides are equal to \(1/4\). Now use the classical identity\[ \sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{m}{2^m}=2. \]Let\[ A=\mathbb N\setminus\{5,6\}, \qquad B=\mathbb N\setminus\{4\}. \]Then\[ \sum_{a\in A}\frac{a}{2^a} = 2-\frac{5}{2^5}-\frac{6}{2^6} = 2-\frac14 = \frac74, \]whereas\[ \sum_{b\in B}\frac{b}{2^b} = 2-\frac{4}{2^4} = 2-\frac14 = \frac74. \]Therefore the rational number\[ x=\frac74 \]has two different representations:\[ \frac74 = \sum_{a\in \mathbb N\setminus\{5,6\}}\frac{a}{2^a} = \sum_{b\in \mathbb N\setminus\{4\}}\frac{b}{2^b}. \]Thus, under this literal interpretation, the weakened question has a positive answer. Of course, this uses two representations with a large common tail; if the intended question requires some stronger non-cancellation or non-degeneracy condition, then that would be a different version.
It is generally believed that $2$ or "two" was a misprint or misread typo and that it should have been $2^{\aleph_0}$.

1) One should add the requirement that $a_1,\ldots,a_t$ are all mutually distinct integers. This is understood in all papers and it is needed to make the problem nontrivial. 2) The remark on the identity stated below the problem (giving representations for infinitely many $n$) is erroneously attributed to me. Later, when I searched the literature, I found exactly the same identity in the paper by Borwein and Loring from 1990. But I think that Cusick should still be credited too. 3) It is still open whether the equation has a solution for all positive integers $n$, but Tengely, Ulas, and Zygadło verified this for $n\leq10000$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
